q: If $[x]$ is the greatest integer $\leq x$, then the value of the integral $\int_{-0.9}^{0.9}\left(\left[x^2\right]+\log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right)\right) d x$ is
question: If $[x]$ is the greatest integer $\leq x$, then the value of the integral $\int_{-0.9}^{0.9}\left(\left[x^2\right]+\log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right)\right) d x$ is
solution:
$$
\text { } \begin{aligned}
& \int_{-0.9}^{0.9}\left\{\left[x^2\right]+\log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right)\right\} d x \\
= & \int_{-0.9}^{0.9}\left[x^2\right] d x+\int_{-0.9}^{0.9} \log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right) d x
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =0+\int_{-0.9}^{0.9} \log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right) d x \\
& \text { Put } x=-x \Rightarrow f(x)=\log \frac{2-x}{2+x} \\
& \text { and } f(-x)=\log \frac{2+x}{2-x} \\
& =-\log \frac{(2-x)}{2+x}=-f(x)
\end{aligned}
$$
So, it is an odd function, hence Required integral $=0$.

q: The value of $\int_0^1 \frac{8 \log (1+x)}{1+x^2} d x$ is
question: The value of $\int_0^1 \frac{8 \log (1+x)}{1+x^2} d x$ is
solution:
$\mathrm{I}=8 \int_0^1 \frac{\log (1+\mathrm{x})}{1+\mathrm{x}^2} \mathrm{dx}$
$=8 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\log (1+\tan \theta)}{1+\tan ^2 \theta} \sec ^2 \theta d \theta(\operatorname{let} x=\tan \theta)$
$=8 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log \left(1+\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right)\right) d \theta=8 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log \left(1+\frac{1-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \theta}\right) d \theta=8 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log 2 d \theta-8 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log (1+\tan \theta) d \theta$
$=8 \log 2 \frac{\pi}{4}-I$
$2 \mathrm{I}=2 \pi \log 2$
$\mathrm{I}=\pi \log 2$

question: The value of $\int_{-1}^1 \frac{(1+\sqrt{|x|-x}) e^x+(\sqrt{|x|-x}) e^{-x}}{e^x+e^{-x}} d x$ is equal to
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}=\int_{-1}^1 \frac{(1+\sqrt{|-\mathrm{x}|-(-\mathrm{x})}) \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}+(\sqrt{|-\mathrm{x}|-(-\mathrm{x})}) \mathrm{e}^{-(-\mathrm{x})}}{\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{e}^{-(-\mathrm{x})}} \mathrm{dx} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\int_{-1}^1 \frac{(1+\sqrt{|\mathrm{x}|+\mathrm{x}}) \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}+(\sqrt{|\mathrm{x}|+\mathrm{x}}) \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}} \mathrm{dx} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}=\int_{-1}^1 \frac{(1+\sqrt{|\mathrm{x}|+\mathrm{x}}+\sqrt{|\mathrm{x}|-\mathrm{x}})\left(\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}\right)}{\left(\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}\right)} \mathrm{dx} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}=\int_{-1}^1(1+\sqrt{|\mathrm{x}|+\mathrm{x}}+\sqrt{|\mathrm{x}|-\mathrm{x}}) \mathrm{dx} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}=2 \int_0^1(1+\sqrt{|\mathrm{x}|+\mathrm{x}}+\sqrt{|\mathrm{x}|-\mathrm{x}}) \mathrm{dx} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}=2 \int_0^1(1+\sqrt{2 \mathrm{x}}+\sqrt{0}) \mathrm{dx} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\int_0^1(1+\sqrt{2 \mathrm{x}}) \mathrm{dx}=\left[\mathrm{x}+\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3} \mathrm{x}^{3 / 2}\right]_0^1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}+1\end{aligned}$

question: The value of $\int_{-\pi}^\pi \frac{2 y(1+\sin y)}{1+\cos ^2 y} d y$ is :
solution:
$\int_{-\pi}^\pi \frac{2 y(1+\sin y)}{1+\cos ^2 y} d y$ \(=\underset{\text{(Odd)}}{\int_{-\pi}^\pi \frac{2 y}{1+\cos ^2 y} d y}+\underset{\text{(Even)}}{\int_{-\pi}^\pi \frac{2 y \sin y}{1+\cos ^2 y} d y}\)
$=0+2.2 \int_0^\pi y\left(\frac{\sin y}{1+\cos ^2 y}\right) d y$ $\begin{aligned} & I=4 \int_0^\pi \frac{y \sin y}{1+\cos ^2 y} d y \\ & I=4 \int_0^\pi \frac{(\pi-y) \sin y}{1+\cos ^2 y} d y \\ & 2 I=4 \int_0^\pi \frac{\pi \sin y}{1+\cos ^2 y} d y \\ & I=2 \pi \int_0^\pi \frac{\sin y}{1+\cos ^2 y} d y \\ & =2 \pi\left(-\tan ^{-1}(\cos y)\right)_0^\pi \\ & =-2 \pi\left[\left(-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)-\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right] \\ & =-2 \pi\left[-\frac{2 \pi}{4}\right]=\pi^2\end{aligned}$

question: The value of $k \in \mathrm{N}$ for which the integral $I_n=\int_0^1\left(1-x^k\right)^n d x, n \in \mathbb{N}$, satisfies $147 I_{20}=148 I_{21}$ is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & I_n=\int_0^1\left(1-x^k\right)^n \cdot 1 d x \\ & I_n=\left(1-x^k\right)^n \cdot x-n k \int_0^1\left(1-x^k\right)^{n-1} \cdot x^{k-1} \cdot d x \\ & I_n=n k \int_0^1\left[\left(1-x^k\right)^n-\left(1-x^k\right)^{n-1}\right] d x \\ & I_n=n k I_n-n k I_n \\ & \frac{I_n}{I_{n-1}}=\frac{n k}{n k+1} \\ & \frac{I_{21}}{I_{20}}=\frac{21 k}{1+21 k} \\ & =\frac{147}{148} \Rightarrow k=7\end{aligned}$

q: Let $(2^{1-a} + 2^{1+a})$, $f(a)$, $(3^a + 3^{-a})$ be in A.P. and $\alpha$ be the minimum value of $f(a)$. Then the value of the integral $\int_{\log_e(\alpha-1)}^{\log_e(\alpha)} \dfrac{dx}{(e^{2x} - e^{-2x})}$ is :
question: Let $(2^{1-a} + 2^{1+a})$, $f(a)$, $(3^a + 3^{-a})$ be in A.P. and $\alpha$ be the minimum value of $f(a)$. Then the value of the integral $\int_{\log_e(\alpha-1)}^{\log_e(\alpha)} \dfrac{dx}{(e^{2x} - e^{-2x})}$ is :
solution:
Since $(2^{1-a} + 2^{1+a})$, $f(a)$, and $(3^a + 3^{-a})$ are in A.P., we have:

$2f(a) = 2^{1-a} + 2^{1+a} + 3^a + 3^{-a}$

$f(a) = (2^{-a} + 2^a) + \dfrac{1}{2}(3^a + 3^{-a})$

Using the AM-GM inequality, $x + \dfrac{1}{x} \ge 2$ for $x > 0$.

$2^a + 2^{-a} \ge 2$ and $3^a + 3^{-a} \ge 2$.

The minimum value of $f(a)$ occurs when $a = 0$.

$\alpha = f(0) = (2^0 + 2^0) + \dfrac{1}{2}(3^0 + 3^0) = 2 + 1 = 3$.

Now, we evaluate the integral:

$I = \int_{\log_e 2}^{\log_e 3} \dfrac{dx}{e^{2x} - e^{-2x}} = \int_{\log_e 2}^{\log_e 3} \dfrac{e^{2x}}{e^{4x} - 1} dx$

Let $e^{2x} = t \Rightarrow 2e^{2x} dx = dt$.

When $x = \log_e 2$, $t = e^{2\log_e 2} = 4$.

When $x = \log_e 3$, $t = e^{2\log_e 3} = 9$.

$I = \dfrac{1}{2} \int_{4}^{9} \dfrac{dt}{t^2 - 1}$

$I = \dfrac{1}{2} \left[ \dfrac{1}{2} \log_e \left| \dfrac{t-1}{t+1} \right| \right]_{4}^{9}$

$I = \dfrac{1}{4} \left( \log_e \dfrac{8}{10} - \log_e \dfrac{3}{5} \right)$

$I = \dfrac{1}{4} \left( \log_e \dfrac{4}{5} - \log_e \dfrac{3}{5} \right) = \dfrac{1}{4} \log_e \left( \dfrac{4/5}{3/5} \right) = \dfrac{1}{4} \log_e \left( \dfrac{4}{3} \right)$

Answer: $\dfrac{1}{4} \log_e\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right)$

q: The value of $\int_0^{20\pi} (\sin^4 x + \cos^4 x) \, dx$ is equal to:
question: The value of $\int_0^{20\pi} (\sin^4 x + \cos^4 x) \, dx$ is equal to:
solution:
Let $I = \int_0^{20\pi} (\sin^4 x + \cos^4 x) \, dx$

Using the algebraic identity $a^2 + b^2 = (a+b)^2 - 2ab$, the integrand can be simplified:

$\sin^4 x + \cos^4 x = (\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x)^2 - 2\sin^2 x \cos^2 x$

Since $\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1$ and $2\sin x \cos x = \sin 2x$:

$\sin^4 x + \cos^4 x = 1 - \dfrac{1}{2} (2\sin x \cos x)^2 = 1 - \dfrac{1}{2} \sin^2 2x$

Using the half-angle formula $\sin^2 \theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$\sin^4 x + \cos^4 x = 1 - \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{1 - \cos 4x}{2} \right) = 1 - \dfrac{1}{4} + \dfrac{1}{4} \cos 4x = \dfrac{3}{4} + \dfrac{1}{4} \cos 4x$

Substituting this back into the integral:

$I = \int_0^{20\pi} \left( \dfrac{3}{4} + \dfrac{1}{4} \cos 4x \right) \, dx$

$I = \left[ \dfrac{3}{4} x + \dfrac{1}{16} \sin 4x \right]_0^{20\pi}$

Evaluating the limits:

$I = \left( \dfrac{3}{4} (20\pi) + \dfrac{1}{16} \sin(80\pi) \right) - (0 + 0)$

Since $\sin(80\pi) = 0$:

$I = 15\pi$

Answer: $15\pi$
